claim_text: The combination of physical examinations with C-reactive protein values do not improve the accuracy of radiographic verified pneumonia predictions.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: CONTRADICT  
The abstract (doc_id 55587) shows that adding C-reactive protein (CRP) values at an optimal cut-off (>30 mg/L) improved the diagnostic accuracy for pneumonia, increasing the area under the ROC curve from 0.70 to 0.77 and enhancing patient classification. This directly contradicts the claim that combining physical examinations with CRP values does not improve prediction accuracy.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
The abstract directly contradicts the claim by demonstrating that adding C-reactive protein (CRP) values to physical examinations improves the diagnostic accuracy of predicting pneumonia in patients presenting with acute cough. The study found that including CRP at a cut-off of >30 mg/L increased the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) from 0.70 to 0.77, and improved net reclassification improvement from 0 to 28%. This suggests that the combination of physical examinations with CRP values does indeed enhance the prediction of pneumonia, contradicting the claim that it does not improve accuracy.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The abstract directly supports the contrary of the claim: it states that "Addition of CRP at the optimal cut off of >30 mg/L increased the ROC area to 0.77 (0.73 to 0.81) and improved the diagnostic classification (net reclassification improvement 28%)." This evidence shows that incorporating C‑reactive protein values with physical examination findings does improve the accuracy of predicting radiographically verified pneumonia.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
The abstract directly contradicts the claim by demonstrating that adding C-reactive protein (CRP) values to physical examinations significantly improves the accuracy of predicting pneumonia in patients presenting to primary care with acute cough. The study found that the optimal combination of clinical prediction items, including absence of runny nose and presence of breathlessness, crackles, diminished breath sounds, tachycardia, and fever, along with CRP >30 mg/L, resulted in an ROC area of 0.77, which is higher than the ROC areas of the individual clinical prediction items alone. This indicates that CRP values do improve the accuracy of radiographic-verified pneumonia predictions when combined with physical examinations.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The abstract (doc_id 5558754) explicitly states that "Addition of CRP at the optimal cut off of >30 mg/L increased the ROC area to 0.77 (0.73 to 0.81) and improved the diagnostic classification (net reclassification improvement 28%).", which directly contradicts the claim that combining physical examinations with CRP values does not improve pneumonia prediction accuracy.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
The abstracts provide evidence that contradicts the claim. The first abstract demonstrates that adding CRP to clinical prediction items improves the diagnostic accuracy of pneumonia in patients presenting with acute cough, increasing the ROC area from 0.70 to 0.77. The second abstract shows that quantitative phosphoproteomic analysis reveals changes in phosphorylation patterns in pazopanib and dasatinib resistant cells, suggesting that these alterations contribute to drug resistance. These findings directly contradict the claim that combining physical examinations with CRP values does not improve the accuracy of radiographic verified pneumonia predictions.
